examples.title: Algorithm: IoT Sensor Data Structure Design
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how to structure IoT sensor data using appropriate data types and organizing them in collections for efficient processing
examples.code:
ALGORITHM IoT_Sensor_Data_Management
INPUT: Raw sensor readings from multiple devices
OUTPUT: Structured sensor data packets ready for transmission

BEGIN
    // Define sensor reading structure
    CREATE STRUCT SensorReading
        device_id: STRING
        timestamp: INTEGER
        temperature: FLOAT
        humidity: FLOAT
        status: STRING
    ENDSTRUCT

    // Initialize data storage
    CREATE LIST readings_history
    CREATE DICT device_registry
    CREATE QUEUE transmit_buffer

    // Function: Create sensor reading packet
    FUNCTION create_reading(device_id, temp, humid, status)
        CREATE new_reading AS SensorReading
        SET new_reading.device_id = device_id
        SET new_reading.timestamp = current_timestamp()
        SET new_reading.temperature = temp
        SET new_reading.humidity = humid
        SET new_reading.status = status
        RETURN new_reading
    ENDFUNCTION

    // Simulate receiving sensor data
    CALL create_reading("SN-101", 25.7, 48.0, "OK")
    ADD result TO readings_history
    ADD result TO transmit_buffer

    // Function: Serialize to JSON format
    FUNCTION to_json(readings_list)
        CREATE empty string json_output
        SET json_output = "{\"device\":\"" + device_id + "\""
        APPEND ",\"timestamp\":\"" + timestamp + "\""
        APPEND ",\"readings\":{\"temperature\":" + temp
        APPEND ",\"humidity\":" + humid + "}}"
        RETURN json_output
    ENDFUNCTION

    // Process readings from queue (FIFO)
    WHILE transmit_buffer IS NOT EMPTY
        SET current_reading = REMOVE_FIRST(transmit_buffer)
        SET json_data = CALL to_json(current_reading)
        PRINT "Transmitting: " + json_data
    ENDWHILE

    PRINT "Data processing complete"
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the fundamental approach to organizing IoT sensor data: defining a structured data type (SensorReading struct), using lists for time-series history storage, dictionaries for device metadata, and queues for FIFO buffering between data collection and transmission tasks. It shows the flow from raw sensor input through structured storage to JSON serialization for network transmission.

examples.title: Python: IoT Sensor Data Handling with Lists and Dictionaries
examples.description: Python implementation showing how to use lists, dictionaries, and classes to handle IoT sensor data, including JSON serialization
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
IoT Sensor Data Handling in Python
Demonstrates fundamental data types and structures for IoT applications
"""
import json
from datetime import datetime
from collections import deque

# ============================================
# 1. FUNDAMENTAL DATA TYPES FOR IoT
# ============================================
print("=== Fundamental IoT Data Types ===")

# Sensor Data (numeric measurements)
temperature = 25.7        # FLOAT - temperature in Celsius
humidity = 48             # INTEGER - humidity percentage
pressure = 1013.2         # FLOAT - pressure in hPa

# Actuator Status (boolean/enum)
actuator_on = True        # BOOLEAN - on/off state
motor_speed = 0           # INTEGER - speed value (0-100)

# Device Metadata (string-based)
device_id = "sensor-node-12"
firmware_version = "v2.1.4"
location = "Building-A-3rd-Floor"

print(f"Device: {device_id}, Temp: {temperature}°C, Humidity: {humidity}%")

# ============================================
# 2. DATA STRUCTURES FOR IoT
# ============================================
print("\n=== Data Structures ===")

# LIST - For time-series sensor readings
temperature_history = [25.5, 25.7, 25.8, 26.0, 26.2]
print(f"Temperature history: {temperature_history}")

# DICT (Dictionary) - For JSON-formatted sensor payload
sensor_payload = {
    "deviceId": "sensor-node-12",
    "timestamp": "2023-10-27T10:15:30Z",
    "readings": {
        "temperature": 25.7,
        "humidity": 48,
        "pressure": 1013.2
    },
    "status": "OK"
}
print(f"Sensor payload: {json.dumps(sensor_payload, indent=2)}")

# SET - For unique device IDs or tags
active_devices = {"SN-101", "SN-102", "SN-103", "SN-101"}  # Duplicate removed
print(f"Active devices: {active_devices}")  # Only unique values

# ============================================
# 3. CLASS FOR SENSOR DATA PACKET
# ============================================
print("\n=== Sensor Reading Class ===")

class SensorReading:
    """Class representing a complete sensor reading packet"""
    
    def __init__(self, device_id, temperature, humidity, timestamp=None):
        self.device_id = device_id
        self.temperature = temperature
        self.humidity = humidity
        self.timestamp = timestamp or datetime.now().isoformat()
    
    def to_dict(self):
        """Convert to dictionary for JSON serialization"""
        return {
            "deviceId": self.device_id,
            "timestamp": self.timestamp,
            "readings": {
                "temperature": self.temperature,
                "humidity": self.humidity
            }
        }
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"SensorReading({self.device_id}, {self.temperature}°C, {self.humidity}%)"

# Create sensor reading objects
reading1 = SensorReading("SN-101", 25.7, 48)
reading2 = SensorReading("SN-102", 26.1, 46)

print(reading1)
print(f"As JSON: {json.dumps(reading1.to_dict(), indent=2)}")

# ============================================
# 4. QUEUE FOR BUFFERING (Producer-Consumer)
# ============================================
print("\n=== Queue Buffer (FIFO) ===")

# Queue for decoupling sensor reading from transmission
transmit_queue = deque(maxlen=100)  # Max 100 readings in buffer

# Simulate data collection (Producer)
def collect_sensor_data(device_id, temp, humid):
    reading = SensorReading(device_id, temp, humid)
    transmit_queue.append(reading)
    print(f"[Producer] Collected: {reading}")

# Simulate data transmission (Consumer)
def transmit_queued_data():
    while transmit_queue:
        reading = transmit_queue.popleft()  # FIFO - remove from left
        json_data = json.dumps(reading.to_dict())
        print(f"[Consumer] Transmitting: {json_data}")

# Simulate the flow
collect_sensor_data("SN-101", 25.5, 48)
collect_sensor_data("SN-102", 26.0, 47)
collect_sensor_data("SN-101", 25.7, 48)

print("\nTransmitting all queued data:")
transmit_queued_data()

# ============================================
# 5. COMPARING DATA STRUCTURES
# ============================================
print("\n=== Data Structure Selection for IoT ===")

# Time-series data -> LIST (ordered, indexable)
time_series = [25.5, 25.6, 25.7, 25.8, 25.9]
print(f"Time-series avg: {sum(time_series)/len(time_series):.2f}°C")

# Device lookup -> DICT (key-value pairs)
device_info = {
    "SN-101": {"location": "Room-A", "type": "temp-humid"},
    "SN-102": {"location": "Room-B", "type": "temp-humid"}
}
print(f"Device SN-101 info: {device_info['SN-101']}")

# Fast membership test -> SET
alert_devices = {"SN-101", "SN-105", "SN-110"}
device_check = "SN-103" in alert_devices
print(f"Is SN-103 in alert list? {device_check}")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates the core data types and structures used in IoT applications: integers and floats for sensor measurements, booleans for actuator states, strings for device metadata; lists for time-series data storage, dictionaries for JSON-formatted payloads, sets for unique device identification, and queues (deque) for buffering between producer and consumer tasks. The SensorReading class shows how to encapsulate sensor data and convert it to JSON format for transmission.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for dictionary/set operations, O(n) for list operations, O(1) for deque append/pop
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of readings stored

examples.title: C: Embedded IoT Data Structures
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating structs, arrays, and buffers commonly used in embedded IoT systems for efficient memory handling
examples.explanation: This C implementation shows the fundamental data structures used in embedded IoT systems: structs for grouping related sensor data (SensorPacket, DeviceInfo), arrays for contiguous time-series storage (SensorBuffer), and a custom queue implementation for FIFO buffering between data collection and network transmission tasks. These patterns are directly applicable to microcontroller programming on devices like Arduino, ESP32, and Raspberry Pi Pico.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for queue enqueue/dequeue, O(n) for array/buffer traversal
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is buffer size (MAX_READINGS)
